title: International trade & exchange-rate systems
summary:
## The big idea Why does Egypt import Japanese cars and export citrus, rather than making everything at home? Because specialisation plus trade lets *both* countries consume **beyond their own PPCs**. The engine of that result — **comparative advantage** — is one of the most counterintuitive and powerful ideas in economics: even a country worse at producing *everything* still gains from trade. This chapter builds that theory, then connects it to how currencies are managed — because every trade flows through an exchange rate. ## Absolute vs comparative advantage - **Absolute advantage**: producing a good with **fewer resources** than another country. Easy — and not the point. - **Comparative advantage** (Ricardo): specialise where your **opportunity cost is lower**. Suppose, with the same resources, Country A can make 4 cars or 8 tonnes of wheat (1 car costs 2 wheat), while Country B can make 1 car or 6 wheat (1 car costs 6 wheat). A is better at *both* — but A's car advantage is bigger. Let A make cars, B grow wheat, and trade at, say, 1 car = 4 wheat: A gets wheat cheaper than making it (4 < the 2... it receives 4 wheat for a car that cost it 2), and B gets cars for 4 wheat instead of 6. **Both gain** — provided the **terms of trade** settle *between* the two opportunity-cost ratios (between 2 and 6 wheat per car). **The small print (your evaluation list):** the model assumes no transport costs, constant returns (no diminishing returns to specialisation), factor immobility between countries but perfect mobility within, and ignores externalities, strategic dependence and who gains *how much*. Real-world comparative advantage also *moves* — it can be created (South Korea built one in ships and chips from nothing). ## The terms of trade > **Terms of trade = (index of export prices ÷ index of import prices) × 100** A **rise** ('improvement') means each unit of exports buys more imports. Careful with words: an *improvement* can hurt if it comes from a currency appreciation pricing your exporters out. For primary-product exporters, the long-run **Prebisch–Singer** tendency is decline — the development chapter's story. ## Free trade: gains and casualties **Gains:** consumption beyond the PPC; lower prices and more variety; **economies of scale** from world-sized markets; competition disciplining domestic monopolies; technology transfer; growth (the post-1950 trade boom lifted East Asia out of poverty). **Casualties:** workers in industries that lose — **structural unemployment** concentrated in regions (the 'China shock' in Western rust belts); infant industries that never get started; over-dependence on volatile world markets; environmental and transport externalities. Free trade grows the pie while redistributing slices — which is why Unit 10's protectionism debate never dies. ## Exchange-rate systems — the menu - **Floating:** the market decides (AS Unit 4's demand/supply diagram). *Pros:* automatic adjustment (a deficit weakens the currency, which helps fix the deficit), no reserves needed, **monetary policy stays free** for domestic goals. *Cons:* volatility deters trade and investment; imported-inflation risk on depreciations. - **Fixed (pegged):** the central bank commits to a rate and defends it — buying/selling **reserves**, moving **interest rates**. Deliberate changes are **devaluation/revaluation**. *Pros:* certainty for traders and investors; an anti-inflation anchor (you import your peg partner's discipline). *Cons:* needs deep reserves; **monetary policy is enslaved** to the peg; pegs invite speculative attack (Switzerland abandoned its euro ceiling in 2015; sterling was blown out of the ERM in 1992). - **Managed float:** mostly market-set, with intervention to smooth swings — the real-world norm (China's yuan the heavyweight example). The **Eurozone** is the extreme fix: one currency, no national monetary policy at all — efficient for trade, brutal for a member in asymmetric crisis (Greece). ## When the currency falls: Marshall–Lerner and the J-curve A depreciation makes exports cheaper and imports dearer — but *volumes* must respond enough to fix the trade balance. The **Marshall–Lerner condition**: a depreciation improves the current account only if > **PED(exports) + PED(imports) > 1** In the **short run** both elasticities are low (contracts signed, habits fixed, no substitutes ready) so the import bill rises with little volume change — the balance **worsens first**. As elasticities rise with time, it improves: the **J-curve**.
**What each part means:** the horizontal line is a **balanced current account (0)**; above it surplus, below it deficit. At the dashed line the currency **depreciates**. Short-run demand for imports and exports is inelastic — the country pays more for much the same imports — so the balance **dips deeper into deficit**. With time, buyers find the now-cheap exports and households substitute away from dear imports: elasticities rise, **Marshall–Lerner** is satisfied, and the balance climbs past its starting point — tracing the letter **J** (orange dot = back to balance).
**Fixing a deficit — two families:** **expenditure-switching** policies redirect spending toward domestic output (depreciation, tariffs), while **expenditure-reducing** policies shrink total spending (tight fiscal/monetary policy — which cuts imports, and everything else with them). Most real adjustment programmes (including IMF packages, Unit 10) mix both. ## Why it matters Comparative advantage is the intellectual case for the open world economy; Marshall–Lerner and the J-curve are the fine print on every devaluation; and fixed-vs-floating is a live policy choice — Egypt's own float of 2016 and after is a textbook J-curve-and-imported-inflation case study.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Absolute** advantage = fewer resources; **comparative** = lower **opportunity cost** — the basis of mutual gains. - Gains require terms of trade **between the two opportunity-cost ratios**; assumptions (no transport costs, constant returns, immobility) = evaluation list. - **Terms of trade** = export price index ÷ import price index × 100. - Free trade: beyond-PPC consumption, scale, competition **vs** structural unemployment, dependence, infant industries. - **Floating** (auto-adjust, policy freedom, volatile) vs **fixed** (certainty, discipline, reserves + policy enslaved, attack-prone) vs **managed float** (the norm). - **Marshall–Lerner**: depreciation improves CA only if PEDx + PEDm > 1; short-run inelasticity → **J-curve**. - Deficit cures: **expenditure-switching** vs **expenditure-reducing** — usually combined.

keyTerms.definition: Producing a good with fewer resources than another country.
Producing a good at a lower opportunity cost than another country; the basis for beneficial trade.
The ratio of export prices to import prices (×100); a rise means exports buy more imports.
A rate determined by market demand and supply for the currency.
A rate pegged and defended by the central bank using reserves and interest rates.
A mainly market-determined rate with occasional central-bank intervention.
A depreciation improves the current account only if the price elasticities of exports and imports sum to more than one.
After a depreciation the trade balance worsens before improving, due to short-run inelastic demand.

diagrams: **Comparative advantage:** a two-country, two-good table/PPC showing opportunity costs and the mutually beneficial terms of trade band.
**FOREX market:** demand and supply of a currency setting the floating rate; show intervention/pegging under a fixed system.
**J-curve:** current-account balance over time, dipping after a depreciation then rising above the start.
tips: Define comparative advantage by **lower opportunity cost** (not fewer resources — that's absolute advantage) and show the **terms-of-trade band** for mutual gains.
Always **evaluate free trade's assumptions** (transport costs, immobility, externalities, over-dependence).
Give a **balanced fixed-vs-floating** comparison (certainty & discipline vs flexibility & policy freedom).
State the **Marshall–Lerner condition** and the **J-curve** when analysing a depreciation's effect on the current account.
Use concrete cases (**China's managed yuan, the Eurozone and Greece, or Switzerland's abandoned franc peg**) to apply devaluation, competitiveness and the fixed-vs-floating trade-off.

channels.name: EconplusDal
channels.why: Strong on Marshall–Lerner, J-curve and fixed vs floating.

revisionNotes:
# Trade & exchange-rate systems — quick revision

**Trade theory**
- Absolute advantage: fewer resources. **Comparative: lower opportunity cost** → specialise → both gain.
- Gains only if **terms of trade lie between the two opportunity-cost ratios**; worked 2×2 example ready.
- Assumptions to attack: no transport costs, constant returns, factor immobility, externalities, dependence.
- **Terms of trade** = (Px index ÷ Pm index) × 100; 'improvement' ≠ always good (appreciation); Prebisch–Singer for primaries.

**Free trade**
- Gains: beyond-PPC consumption, scale economies, competition, technology, growth.
- Costs: structural/regional unemployment, infant industries, over-dependence, environment.

**Exchange-rate systems**
- **Floating**: auto-adjustment, no reserves, free monetary policy; volatile, imported inflation.
- **Fixed**: certainty + inflation anchor; needs reserves, monetary policy captured, speculative attacks (ERM '92, Swiss '15). Deliberate moves = de/revaluation.
- **Managed float**: the real-world norm (yuan). Eurozone = ultimate fix (Greece = the cost).

**Depreciation analysis**
- **Marshall–Lerner**: improves CA only if **PEDx + PEDm > 1**.
- **J-curve**: short-run inelasticity → worse first, better later.
- Deficit cures: expenditure-**switching** (depreciation, tariffs) + expenditure-**reducing** (deflation) — mixed in practice (IMF programmes; Egypt 2016 float).
questions:
# Practice questions — International trade & exchange-rate systems **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** With the same resources, Country P can produce 10 phones or 20 shirts; Country Q can produce 4 phones or 16 shirts. Which statement is correct? - A — P has a comparative advantage in shirts - B — Q has a comparative advantage in shirts - C — Q has an absolute advantage in phones - D — no mutually beneficial trade is possible
▸ Show answer

B. Opportunity cost of 1 phone: P = 2 shirts, Q = 4 shirts; of 1 shirt: P = 0.5 phones, Q = 0.25 phones. Q sacrifices less for shirts → Q specialises in shirts, P in phones; trade between 2 and 4 shirts per phone benefits both.

**2.** A country's export price index rises from 100 to 110 while its import price index rises from 100 to 121. Its terms of trade have: - A — improved by 10% - B — deteriorated by about 9% - C — improved by 21% - D — stayed constant
▸ Show answer

B. ToT = 110/121 × 100 ≈ 90.9 — a fall of ~9%: each unit of exports now buys fewer imports.

**3.** Under a fixed exchange-rate system, a central bank facing downward pressure on its currency will typically: - A — cut interest rates and sell its own currency - B — raise interest rates and buy its own currency using reserves - C — abandon monetary policy entirely - D — impose a maximum price on foreign currency
▸ Show answer

B. Defending a peg means propping up demand for the currency: buy it with foreign reserves and raise rates to attract capital — which is exactly why a peg 'enslaves' monetary policy.

**4.** The Marshall–Lerner condition states that a depreciation improves the current account only if: - A — the sum of the price elasticities of demand for exports and imports exceeds one - B — export prices rise faster than import prices - C — the government reduces spending - D — demand for imports is perfectly inelastic
▸ Show answer

A. PEDx + PEDm > 1 — volumes must respond strongly enough to outweigh the worsened price effect; short-run failure of this condition produces the J-curve dip.

## Paper 4 style — data response > Facing a chronic current account deficit and dwindling reserves, a country abandons its long-standing dollar peg and floats. The currency loses 45% overnight. In the following year: inflation jumps from 6% to 22%; the trade deficit widens for nine months, then narrows sharply; textile and food-processing exports rise 30%; tourism revives; and the central bank — freed from defending the peg — raises interest rates to fight inflation. The IMF supports the programme with a loan tied to fiscal tightening. **(a)** Distinguish between a devaluation and a depreciation. **[2]** **(b)** Explain why the country's central bank could not previously use interest rates to manage domestic inflation. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse the behaviour of the trade balance over the year, using the Marshall–Lerner condition and the J-curve. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether abandoning the peg has left the country better off. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Devaluation = deliberate lowering of a fixed/pegged rate by the authorities; depreciation = a market-driven fall under floating. [1+1]

(b) Defending the peg dictated monetary policy: rates had to serve the exchange-rate target (attracting capital, protecting reserves), not domestic goals — the fixed-rate loss of monetary independence. [4]

(c) First nine months: import demand (fuel, food, machinery) and export contracts inelastic → import bill up 45% in local terms, volumes static → deficit widens (J-curve's dip; Marshall–Lerner unmet short run). Then elasticities rise: exports +30%, tourism revives, import substitution → condition satisfied → sharp narrowing (the J's upstroke). [6]

(d) Better: adjustment mechanism restored, exports and tourism booming, reserves no longer bleeding, monetary policy freed (already in use against inflation). Worse: 22% inflation savages real incomes — the poor hardest hit; IMF austerity compounds short-run pain. Judgement: the peg was unsustainable (reserves dwindling) — the float front-loads pain for a workable external position; success depends on taming inflation. (Egypt's 2016 float fits this template.) [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain, with a numerical example, how two countries can both gain from trade even when one has an absolute advantage in every good. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether the theory of comparative advantage justifies completely free trade in the real world. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) 2×2 opportunity-cost table; identify comparative advantages; choose terms of trade between the ratios; show both consume beyond autarky.

(b) For: gains from specialisation, scale, competition — the postwar trade-and-growth record. Against: assumptions fail (transport, diminishing returns), adjustment costs and regional scarring, infant industries, strategic dependence (chips, food, energy), distribution of gains, environment. Judgement: strong presumption for openness, tempered by transition support and targeted exceptions — not a proof of laissez-faire.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain the advantages and disadvantages of a fixed exchange-rate system. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether a floating exchange rate will automatically correct a current account deficit. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Certainty for trade/FDI, anti-inflation anchor, discipline vs reserve requirements, loss of monetary independence, speculative attacks, misalignment risk.

(b) Mechanism: deficit → currency supply exceeds demand → depreciation → competitiveness restored. Qualifications: Marshall–Lerner may fail short run (J-curve); capital flows can prop up a deficit currency for years (financial account dominance); imported inflation erodes the gain; structural deficits (competitiveness, quality) don't yield to price alone. Judgement: a tendency, not an automatic fix.
